title: Surgical Site Infection
oneLiner: Post-operative wound infection (superficial or deep) within 30 days (or 1 year if implant) — delays recovery, can cause sepsis or dehiscence.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Necrotising fasciitis / gas gangrene** — rapidly spreading pain out of proportion to exam, crepitus on palpation, systemic toxicity (tachycardia, delirium). Single finding: **subcutaneous gas on X-ray or CT** – call senior + microbiology immediately; the urgency is surgical debridement, not antibiotics alone.
- **Sepsis / septic shock** — fever/hypothermia, hypotension, rising lactate, altered mental state. Single finding: **lactate >2 mmol/L + wound erythema/purulence** – start antibiotics within 1 hour (after blood cultures) and escalate to critical care if vasopressors required.
- **Wound dehiscence with evisceration** — sudden gush of serosanguinous fluid, visible bowel/omentum. **Do not reduce; cover with saline-soaked gauze, call surgical registrar immediately** – this is a surgical emergency.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** wound pain increasing >24h post-op, local warmth, redness, swelling, purulent or serous discharge with foul odour.
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Erythema extending >2 cm from wound edge  
  - Induration, fluctuance (abscess)  
  - Gap in wound closure (dehiscence)  
  - Fever (may be absent)  
  - Lymphangitis (red streaks up limb) – suggests streptococcal infection
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **SSI (superficial)** – erythema, purulent discharge, wound edge separation – most common.
- **SSI (deep/organ space)** – fever, systemic symptoms, intra-abdominal collection (US/CT will show fluid collection adjacent to surgical site).
- **Wound haematoma** – bruised, tense, non-erythematous; no purulence.
- **Cellulitis** (without wound involvement) – diffuse erythema and oedema starting away from wound; no discharge.
- **Contact dermatitis** (e.g. from dressing, chlorhexidine) – pruritic, vesicular, no fever.
- **Suture abscess** – small localised pustule at a single stitch; no systemic signs.
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – see Killers First.

**Splitter:** *Purulent discharge on wound inspection or expressible pus → SSI (superficial or deep). No pus but spreading cellulitis with systemic malaise → consider deeper infection or early cellulitis.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Wound inspection and gentle expression; take **swab for MC&S** (aerobic + anaerobic) from deep part of wound. If no obvious pus, use wound aspirate or saline-moistened swab from erythematous margin.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WCC, CRP), renal function, lactate, blood cultures if fever/signs of sepsis.
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** Wound US (if abscess suspected) – confirms fluid collection depth.  
  - **Second-line:** CT scan if deep/organ-space infection suspected (e.g. post abdominal surgery, hip/knee arthroplasty).  
  - **If gas gangrene suspected:** plain X-ray or CT – look for gas tracks.
- **Special tests:** Consider tissue biopsy if chronic infection or atypical organism (especially after implant surgery).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **CDC/NHSN SSI criteria** – superficial incisional (skin/subcutaneous), deep incisional (fascia/muscle), organ/space. *Not a scoring system but helpful classification for severity and management.*
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures before first dose of antibiotics** in any patient with fever, rigors, or signs of sepsis.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED / ward):**  
  - If signs of sepsis: ABCDE, IV fluids, blood cultures, start empiric antibiotics (see Drugs).  
  - If wound fluctuant + purulent: **open the wound** (incision and drainage) – do this under sterile conditions, send pus for culture.  
  - If suspected necrotising fasciitis: call surgical registrar + microbiologist immediately; do not wait for imaging – go to theatre.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**  
  - Daily wound assessment: measure erythema, record presence/absence of discharge.  
  - Wound care: if opened, **pack with damp saline gauze** (or alginate/gel dressings) to allow healing by secondary intention; change dressing daily or as per output.  
  - De-escalate antibiotics based on culture results.  
  - If deep infection with implant (e.g. joint prosthesis): consult orthopaedic/microbiology – may need removal of implant.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Continue wound dressings.  
  - If healing slow (e.g. >2 weeks), consider negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) or wound clinic referral.  
  - For recurrent SSI: rule out retained suture material, sinus tract, or underlying immunosuppression.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride IV 1L over 8-12h (if not eating/drinking).  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if no contraindication, and not actively bleeding).  
  - **Diet:** normal unless NBM for theatre.  
  - **Observations:** NEWS 4-hourly; if sepsis, consider 1-hourly until stable.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (empiric, mild-moderate, no sepsis):**  
  - Fluconazole? No – fungal rarely first-line.  
  - **Flucloxacillin** 500 mg – 1 g QDS oral or IV (depending on severity).  
  - If penicillin allergic: **Clindamycin** 300–600 mg QDS oral/IV.
- **If sepsis/severe infection (IV route):**  
  - **Co-amoxiclav** 1.2 g TDS IV (adults).  
  - Or **Piperacillin-tazobactam** 4.5 g QDS IV (for hospital-acquired or complex patients).  
  - Add **Gentamicin** (single daily dose, e.g. 5–7 mg/kg IV, check local policy) if Gram-negative cover needed (e.g. colorectal surgery).  
  - If MRSA risk (known colonisation, previous MRSA, prolonged hospitalisation): add **Vancomycin** (IV, TDM guided) or **Teicoplanin** (IV/IM, check local protocol).
- **Key contraindications:** Penicillin allergy (use alternatives). Gentamicin – avoid or adjust in renal impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Gentamicin, vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam all require dose adjustment per eGFR. [Check local policy for specific doses.]
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Gentamicin:** *Never give a second dose before checking trough level and renal function* – risk of ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity.  
  - **Vancomycin:** *Infuse over ≥1 hour (painful phlebitis risk) and monitor levels* – leading cause of AKI if given without TDM.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Wound dehiscence (may lead to evisceration).  
  - Sepsis / septic shock.  
  - Necrotising fasciitis (rare but devastating).  
  - Abscess formation requiring repeat drainage.
- **Chronic:**  
  - Sinus tract formation (persistent discharge).  
  - Chronic wound / non-healing ulcer.  
  - Implant infection (joint prosthesis, mesh) requiring removal.  
  - Scar contracture.
sections.Prognosis:
- Superficial SSI: usually resolves with drainage + antibiotics within 5-10 days.  
- Deep/organ-space SSI: longer course (weeks), higher risk of recurrence, implant removal may be needed – doubling recovery time.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Take a photo of the wound at first assessment** – allows objective comparison tomorrow.  
- **Document CDC type (superficial/deep/organ-space) and size** – helps guide antibiotic duration.  
- **Always check wound edges for dehiscence** – even a small gap can be a portal for deeper infection.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Closing a drained abscess with sutures** – must leave open to heal by secondary intention.  
- **Withholding antibiotics until swab result** in septic patient – start empiric treatment after cultures.  
- **Not checking for foreign body (e.g. retained gauze/swab)** – particularly after previous surgery; CT or re-exploration may be needed.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - Pain worsens despite analgesia or spreading redness beyond wound edge.  
  - New fever or rigors.  
  - Wound opens, or discharge becomes pus/foul-smelling.
- **Follow-up:**  
  - District nurse or wound clinic for dressing changes (usually every 1-2 days).  
  - Surgical clinic review in 1-2 weeks (or earlier if symptoms not settling).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call surgical registrar immediately (or bleep 2222 for crash team) if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥6 or rising lactate >2 mmol/L uncontrollable with IV fluids.  
  - Crepitus in wound/overlying skin.  
  - Signs of dehiscence with evisceration.  
  - Spreading cellulitis >5 cm from wound edge despite IV antibiotics.  
- **Call microbiology for** MRSA, polymicrobial, or implant-associated infections – choose antibiotics carefully.

rawMarkdown:
## Surgical Site Infection
**One-liner:** Post-operative wound infection (superficial or deep) within 30 days (or 1 year if implant) — delays recovery, can cause sepsis or dehiscence.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Necrotising fasciitis / gas gangrene** — rapidly spreading pain out of proportion to exam, crepitus on palpation, systemic toxicity (tachycardia, delirium). Single finding: **subcutaneous gas on X-ray or CT** – call senior + microbiology immediately; the urgency is surgical debridement, not antibiotics alone.
- **Sepsis / septic shock** — fever/hypothermia, hypotension, rising lactate, altered mental state. Single finding: **lactate >2 mmol/L + wound erythema/purulence** – start antibiotics within 1 hour (after blood cultures) and escalate to critical care if vasopressors required.
- **Wound dehiscence with evisceration** — sudden gush of serosanguinous fluid, visible bowel/omentum. **Do not reduce; cover with saline-soaked gauze, call surgical registrar immediately** – this is a surgical emergency.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** wound pain increasing >24h post-op, local warmth, redness, swelling, purulent or serous discharge with foul odour.
- **Key exam findings:**  
  - Erythema extending >2 cm from wound edge  
  - Induration, fluctuance (abscess)  
  - Gap in wound closure (dehiscence)  
  - Fever (may be absent)  
  - Lymphangitis (red streaks up limb) – suggests streptococcal infection

### Differential Diagnosis
- **SSI (superficial)** – erythema, purulent discharge, wound edge separation – most common.
- **SSI (deep/organ space)** – fever, systemic symptoms, intra-abdominal collection (US/CT will show fluid collection adjacent to surgical site).
- **Wound haematoma** – bruised, tense, non-erythematous; no purulence.
- **Cellulitis** (without wound involvement) – diffuse erythema and oedema starting away from wound; no discharge.
- **Contact dermatitis** (e.g. from dressing, chlorhexidine) – pruritic, vesicular, no fever.
- **Suture abscess** – small localised pustule at a single stitch; no systemic signs.
- **Necrotising fasciitis** – see Killers First.

**Splitter:** *Purulent discharge on wound inspection or expressible pus → SSI (superficial or deep). No pus but spreading cellulitis with systemic malaise → consider deeper infection or early cellulitis.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Wound inspection and gentle expression; take **swab for MC&S** (aerobic + anaerobic) from deep part of wound. If no obvious pus, use wound aspirate or saline-moistened swab from erythematous margin.
- **Bloods:** FBC (WCC, CRP), renal function, lactate, blood cultures if fever/signs of sepsis.
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** Wound US (if abscess suspected) – confirms fluid collection depth.  
  - **Second-line:** CT scan if deep/organ-space infection suspected (e.g. post abdominal surgery, hip/knee arthroplasty).  
  - **If gas gangrene suspected:** plain X-ray or CT – look for gas tracks.
- **Special tests:** Consider tissue biopsy if chronic infection or atypical organism (especially after implant surgery).
- **Diagnostic criteria:** **CDC/NHSN SSI criteria** – superficial incisional (skin/subcutaneous), deep incisional (fascia/muscle), organ/space. *Not a scoring system but helpful classification for severity and management.*
- **Never-miss test:** **Blood cultures before first dose of antibiotics** in any patient with fever, rigors, or signs of sepsis.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED / ward):**  
  - If signs of sepsis: ABCDE, IV fluids, blood cultures, start empiric antibiotics (see Drugs).  
  - If wound fluctuant + purulent: **open the wound** (incision and drainage) – do this under sterile conditions, send pus for culture.  
  - If suspected necrotising fasciitis: call surgical registrar + microbiologist immediately; do not wait for imaging – go to theatre.
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**  
  - Daily wound assessment: measure erythema, record presence/absence of discharge.  
  - Wound care: if opened, **pack with damp saline gauze** (or alginate/gel dressings) to allow healing by secondary intention; change dressing daily or as per output.  
  - De-escalate antibiotics based on culture results.  
  - If deep infection with implant (e.g. joint prosthesis): consult orthopaedic/microbiology – may need removal of implant.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Continue wound dressings.  
  - If healing slow (e.g. >2 weeks), consider negative pressure wound therapy (NPWT) or wound clinic referral.  
  - For recurrent SSI: rule out retained suture material, sinus tract, or underlying immunosuppression.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - **Fluids:** 0.9% sodium chloride IV 1L over 8-12h (if not eating/drinking).  
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (if no contraindication, and not actively bleeding).  
  - **Diet:** normal unless NBM for theatre.  
  - **Observations:** NEWS 4-hourly; if sepsis, consider 1-hourly until stable.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (empiric, mild-moderate, no sepsis):**  
  - Fluconazole? No – fungal rarely first-line.  
  - **Flucloxacillin** 500 mg – 1 g QDS oral or IV (depending on severity).  
  - If penicillin allergic: **Clindamycin** 300–600 mg QDS oral/IV.
- **If sepsis/severe infection (IV route):**  
  - **Co-amoxiclav** 1.2 g TDS IV (adults).  
  - Or **Piperacillin-tazobactam** 4.5 g QDS IV (for hospital-acquired or complex patients).  
  - Add **Gentamicin** (single daily dose, e.g. 5–7 mg/kg IV, check local policy) if Gram-negative cover needed (e.g. colorectal surgery).  
  - If MRSA risk (known colonisation, previous MRSA, prolonged hospitalisation): add **Vancomycin** (IV, TDM guided) or **Teicoplanin** (IV/IM, check local protocol).
- **Key contraindications:** Penicillin allergy (use alternatives). Gentamicin – avoid or adjust in renal impairment.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** Gentamicin, vancomycin, piperacillin-tazobactam all require dose adjustment per eGFR. [Check local policy for specific doses.]
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Gentamicin:** *Never give a second dose before checking trough level and renal function* – risk of ototoxicity/nephrotoxicity.  
  - **Vancomycin:** *Infuse over ≥1 hour (painful phlebitis risk) and monitor levels* – leading cause of AKI if given without TDM.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Wound dehiscence (may lead to evisceration).  
  - Sepsis / septic shock.  
  - Necrotising fasciitis (rare but devastating).  
  - Abscess formation requiring repeat drainage.
- **Chronic:**  
  - Sinus tract formation (persistent discharge).  
  - Chronic wound / non-healing ulcer.  
  - Implant infection (joint prosthesis, mesh) requiring removal.  
  - Scar contracture.

### Prognosis
- Superficial SSI: usually resolves with drainage + antibiotics within 5-10 days.  
- Deep/organ-space SSI: longer course (weeks), higher risk of recurrence, implant removal may be needed – doubling recovery time.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Take a photo of the wound at first assessment** – allows objective comparison tomorrow.  
- **Document CDC type (superficial/deep/organ-space) and size** – helps guide antibiotic duration.  
- **Always check wound edges for dehiscence** – even a small gap can be a portal for deeper infection.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Closing a drained abscess with sutures** – must leave open to heal by secondary intention.  
- **Withholding antibiotics until swab result** in septic patient – start empiric treatment after cultures.  
- **Not checking for foreign body (e.g. retained gauze/swab)** – particularly after previous surgery; CT or re-exploration may be needed.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**  
  - Pain worsens despite analgesia or spreading redness beyond wound edge.  
  - New fever or rigors.  
  - Wound opens, or discharge becomes pus/foul-smelling.
- **Follow-up:**  
  - District nurse or wound clinic for dressing changes (usually every 1-2 days).  
  - Surgical clinic review in 1-2 weeks (or earlier if symptoms not settling).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call surgical registrar immediately (or bleep 2222 for crash team) if:**  
  - NEWS2 ≥6 or rising lactate >2 mmol/L uncontrollable with IV fluids.  
  - Crepitus in wound/overlying skin.  
  - Signs of dehiscence with evisceration.  
  - Spreading cellulitis >5 cm from wound edge despite IV antibiotics.  
- **Call microbiology for** MRSA, polymicrobial, or implant-associated infections – choose antibiotics carefully.
